blocks.text: Dear Ms. Hubbard:

This letter confirms the terms on which we will study the funding policy for the retirement plan sponsored by {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}}. It records the work we have agreed to do, what we will rely on, the standards the work is performed under, and the commercial terms. If it states our understanding correctly, please countersign and return a copy.
Background and Purpose

Wells, Walsh and Martinez has asked us to examine how contributions to the plan are presently determined, and whether that practice, continued, is likely to fund the promised benefits on an orderly schedule. Our understanding is that the firm has nothing set down in writing and that the contribution is decided year by year against the range indicated in the valuation report. That is common and not, by itself, a deficiency. It is hard to defend when the number moves.

A funding policy is a decision about how to pay for a promise over time. It is not a measurement and it is not a forecast, though it depends on both. We will keep the three apart: what the plan's data and current assumptions show; what we assumed in projecting the alternatives forward; and what, on that basis, we recommend. Where a recommendation rests on judgment rather than measurement, we will name the judgment.
Scope of Work
The study proceeds in five parts. We expect to review the first two with your staff before the projection work begins; a projection built on a misunderstood current practice is worse than none.

items: Review of current practice: the cost method, the asset valuation method and any smoothing, the treatment of the unfunded liability and the period over which it is being paid down, and, separately, the extent to which the amount contributed has followed the amount indicated.

Statement of objectives. Working with you, we will set down what the firm wants the policy to achieve and in what order of priority. Benefit security, contribution stability, and equity between partner cohorts pull against one another; a statement that claims to maximize all three has not been thought through.
Candidate policies: no fewer than three, each satisfying the objectives to a differing degree, and each expressed as a rule that can be applied in a year when we are not engaged.

Projections of the funded position and the contribution under each candidate, deterministically on the current assumptions and on stressed variants, and stochastically where the distribution of outcomes rather than a single path is the question.
Recommendation and a written policy the firm can adopt, with the disclosures it should carry and the cadence on which it is reviewed.

blocks.text: Outside this engagement, and available under a separate letter: the annual valuation and any opinion arising from it; benefit calculations for individual participants; plan design studies; advice on the investment of plan assets, on which we do not opine; auditor disclosure schedules; and any communication addressed to a party other than the firm and the plan's fiduciaries.
Information We Will Rely On

Our work relies on information furnished by the firm and its service providers: the participant census; the custodian's statements; the plan document and every amendment in effect; the recent valuation reports and their exhibits, whoever prepared them; and the contribution history. We will review what we receive for internal consistency and general reasonableness, and we will tell you promptly if something looks wrong. We will not audit it. If it is materially incomplete or inaccurate our results will be affected accordingly, and we do not accept responsibility for that. Where an item cannot be obtained we will assume, and disclose both the assumption and why we made it.
Professional Standards

The work will be performed in accordance with the Actuarial Standards of Practice promulgated by the Actuarial Standards Board, by actuaries who meet the applicable qualification standards. Robert Lawrence is responsible for the engagement and signs any statement of actuarial opinion arising from it. The revised standard on measuring pension obligations takes effect this year; a policy study is not a funding valuation, but we will reflect it where it bears on how the measurements we use reach you.
The Engagement Team

Robert Lawrence, our Managing Actuary, is responsible for the work and will present the results. Michelle Banks, Actuarial Associate, will run the study day to day and will be your ordinary point of contact; her working papers document each step so a reviewer can retrace it. David Sherman, Senior Analyst, will reconcile the data and build the projection model. We understand that requests for information will go through Lisa Hubbard's office. We will not add a person to this engagement without telling you who and why.
Fee and Commencement

Our fee for the work described above is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}. It is a fixed fee for the scope stated here; it is not an estimate and it does not vary with the hours we spend. Subject to your countersignature, we will commence on {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}} and expect the work to run approximately four months from that date, assuming the information arrives substantially as requested. Where a schedule of this kind slips, the cause is ordinarily the data rather than the analysis.
Fees and Payment

We will invoice the fixed fee in four equal monthly installments beginning in the first month, payable within thirty days of receipt. Reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, principally travel to meetings you ask us to attend in person, are billed at cost and are not included in the fixed fee. Work outside the stated scope will be performed only on your written authorization, at our standard hourly rates, quoted before it begins.
Term and Termination

The engagement begins on the commencement date stated above and continues until we deliver the recommendation and the draft policy, or until terminated. Either party may terminate on thirty days' written notice, with or without cause. On termination you will pay the portion of the fixed fee corresponding to the work performed to that date, and we will deliver the work product as it then stands, with its incompleteness disclosed on its face.
Confidentiality

We will treat the information you furnish as confidential and will not disclose it except to our personnel working on the engagement, to a party you authorize in writing, or where required by law, professional standards, or legal process, in which case we will notify you beforehand if permitted. This does not extend to information that is public or that we receive from a third party without restriction. Our working papers remain our property. This paragraph survives the engagement.
Limitation of Liability

Our work is prepared for the firm and the plan's fiduciaries, for the purpose stated here, and no other party may rely on it. Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees paid to us under it, except for our fraud or willful misconduct. Neither party is liable to the other for consequential, indirect, or punitive damages. Nothing we deliver guarantees any funded position, contribution, or investment result; actual experience will differ from every projection we prepare, by amounts we cannot know in advance.
Governing Law

This engagement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office is located, without regard to its conflict of laws rules, and any dispute arising out of it will be brought in the courts of that state.
Acceptance

If this letter sets out our agreement, please sign below and return a copy to us; the countersigned letter is the entire agreement between us on this engagement. If anything here reads differently to you than it was discussed, tell us before you sign rather than after.
Kickoff Memo: Funding Policy Study

To: David Sherman
From: Michelle Banks
Re: {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} funding policy study, kickoff and workstreams

The letter is countersigned and we have been on the clock since {{fact:f:E-2023-001.start}}. What Wells, Walsh and Martinez has asked for is a written funding policy: a rule that says how much the firm contributes to the plan in a given year and why, that can be applied in a year when we are not in the room, and that the partners can defend to each other when the number goes up. Robert Lawrence is responsible for it and will present it. He will want the study to say what the plan's own data show, then what we assumed in order to project it forward, then what we recommend, in that order and in separate paragraphs. We should build the working papers that way from the first week rather than reorganize them in the last one.

It is worth being clear about what this is not, because the question will come up. We are not performing the plan's annual valuation and we are not certifying anything. We are not advising on how the assets are invested; if that is asked, the answer is that it is outside our scope and outside our qualification, not that we will take a look. We are not designing benefits. Anything that arrives in a hallway gets scoped in writing before it gets worked on.
Workstreams

items: Current practice. David Sherman owns this. Document how the contribution has actually been determined: the cost method, the asset valuation method and any smoothing, and how the unfunded liability is being amortized and over what period. Separately, and this is the part that matters, what was contributed each year against what was indicated for that year. If those two diverge we write down that they diverge; we do not average it into a sentence about general consistency.

Data. Also David Sherman. Census, custodian statements, plan document and every amendment, the recent valuation reports with their exhibits, and the contribution history. Reconcile the census against the population in the most recent valuation before anything is built on it. If the extract is quietly a different population than the report describes, that is a finding, and it is due in the first two weeks rather than the last two.

Objectives. I will draft the objectives statement and take it to Lisa Hubbard, and I would rather have the firm's priorities in her words than in ours. Benefit security, contribution stability, and equity across partner cohorts trade against one another. A draft that records the firm wanting all three equally is a draft I have not finished.

Candidates and projections. Three alternatives at minimum, each written as a rule rather than as a figure. David Sherman builds the projection model and I specify the scenarios. Deterministic on the current assumptions and on stressed variants first; stochastic only where the spread of outcomes rather than a single path is what the decision turns on. Every assumption goes on the model's assumption sheet, not buried in a formula where a reviewer has to find it.

Recommendation and draft policy. I write it and Robert Lawrence reviews it before it goes anywhere. It ends with a policy the firm can adopt as written, including how often it is reviewed and what would trigger a review off cycle.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: David sends the itemized data request to Lisa Hubbard this week, with the field definitions attached so nobody on either side is guessing at codes.

I will ask Lisa Hubbard who at the firm decides the contribution today, and whether anything about that decision is already written down somewhere. If there is a memo in a drawer we would rather read it than reconstruct it.
David flags any gap in the amendment history before we assume the history we received is complete.
I will put the internal review with Robert Lawrence on the calendar now, far enough out that it can be a review rather than a signature.
No number leaves this team, in any form, until the census reconciliation is signed off. That includes a number in a sentence that begins with the word roughly.

blocks.text: We are at the beginning, which is the cheapest point at which to discover that we have misunderstood the question. If any of the above reads differently to you than it does to me, say so this week.
Working Session Minutes: Funding Policy Study

Re: {{fact:f:E-2023-001.client}} funding policy study, working session
Recorded by: David Sherman
Attendees

items: Michelle Banks, Actuarial Associate
David Sherman, Senior Analyst
Lisa Hubbard, Chief Operating Officer, Wells, Walsh and Martinez

blocks.text: Discussion

Session ran ninety minutes at the client's offices. Purpose was to walk through the current-practice findings and move the objectives statement toward something the firm will sign. Minutes below record what was said and what was agreed. Where they record a view rather than a decision, that is marked.

Current practice. Documented and circulated ahead of the session: the cost method, the asset valuation method and the smoothing it applies, and the amortization of the unfunded liability with the period over which it runs. Lisa Hubbard had no corrections to the description. She asked whether the amortization period was chosen or inherited and the honest answer is that the material we have does not say. Noted as an open question rather than guessed at.

Census. Reconciled against the population in the most recent valuation report. The two differ by nine records and each one is accounted for: seven terminations processed after the extract date, two rehires appearing twice. No liability is affected. Raised here because an unexplained difference found in review later costs more than an explained one recorded now.

Contribution history. This is the finding. Across the years covered by the material we were given, the amount contributed sits at the low end of the indicated range in some years and the high end in others, and the pattern follows the firm's own results rather than anything about the plan. Michelle Banks made the point that this is a description and not a criticism: absent a written policy there was no rule to depart from. Lisa Hubbard did not dispute the pattern. She said the decision is made each year in the compensation committee with the valuation report in front of it and no written standard applied to it.

Objectives. Lisa Hubbard ranked benefit security first and contribution stability second, and said the firm can live with a contribution that is higher on average if it is predictable. Equity across partner cohorts came up and was not resolved. Michelle Banks noted that the ranking as stated will steer the candidates toward a fixed-percentage rule with a corridor, and that the firm ought to see what that costs in a good year before it commits rather than after. Agreed the objectives statement stays in draft until the projections are run against it.

Assumptions. Nothing recommended and nothing changed. Projections will use the assumptions in the most recent valuation as the base case, with stressed variants around the discount rate and mortality improvement. For the record: the base case is what we were given, the variants are ours, and neither one is a forecast.
Open data. The amendment the plan document references but that is not in what we received, and the custodian's statements for one quarter. Neither blocks the model. Both block the report.
Next session in three weeks, on the candidate policies. Lisa Hubbard asked whether the projections can be shown as a range rather than as a line. They can, and they will be.
Action Items

rows: Circulate the current-practice memo with the census reconciliation appended
Revise the objectives statement to reflect the ranking; hold in draft
Produce the missing amendment and the outstanding custodian statements
Specify the three candidate policies as written rules, not figures
Run deterministic projections on the base case and the stressed variants
Confirm who adopts the policy: the compensation committee or the partners

blocks.text: Status Report: Funding Policy Study
Summary

The study of the plan's funding policy is at roughly its midpoint and is on schedule. The current-practice review is complete, the census has been reconciled, and the objectives statement is drafted and with your office. The projection model is built and the base case runs. The candidate policies are specified and are being priced now. Nothing we have found so far changes our view that the work can be delivered within the scope and on the schedule set out in the engagement letter. Two data items remain open. Neither is holding up the analysis, and both will hold up the report if they stay open. That distinction is one this report will keep making.
Completed This Period

items: Current practice documented. How the plan's contribution has been determined is now written down: the cost method, the asset valuation method and the smoothing it applies, and the amortization of the unfunded liability with the period over which it is being paid down. This is a description of what has been done. It is not a criticism of it, and it should not be read as one.

Census reconciled. David Sherman reconciled the participant extract against the population in the most recent valuation report. The two differ by nine records, each of which is individually explained: seven terminations processed after the extract date and two rehires appearing twice. No liability is affected. We record it because an unexplained difference discovered later is expensive and an explained one is not.

Contribution history assembled. The amounts actually contributed are now set against the amounts indicated for the same years. They diverge, and the divergence tracks the firm's own results rather than anything about the plan. We have measured this. We have not yet formed a view on it, and we would rather you saw the measurement before you saw our view.

Objectives drafted. Following the working session with your office, the statement records benefit security first and contribution stability second, with equity across partner cohorts identified and unresolved. It stays in draft until the projections show what the ranking costs.
Projection model built. It runs on the assumptions in the most recent valuation as its base case. The base case is what we were given. Anything we vary from it will be labeled in the report as ours.

blocks.text: Work Underway and Upcoming

items: Price the three candidate policies deterministically, on the base case and on stressed variants around the discount rate and mortality improvement.

Run the stochastic work, limited to the questions where the spread of outcomes rather than a single path is what the decision turns on. We do not intend to run it where it would add decimal places and nothing else.
Resolve the equity question with your office, or record it as unresolved and show what each candidate does to it. Either is an acceptable outcome. Leaving it unstated is not.
Draft the written policy in adoptable form, including the review cadence and the conditions that would trigger a review off cycle.
Internal review by Robert Lawrence before any of it is presented to the firm.

blocks.text: Budget

The fixed fee for this engagement is {{fact:f:E-2023-001.fee}}. We are approximately half through the fee and approximately half through the work, and we do not expect to ask for more. The scope has not changed and nothing has arisen that we would characterize as outside it. If the missing amendment turns out to alter the plan's provisions in a way the recent valuations did not reflect, that would be a scope conversation, and it is one we would start with you before doing the work rather than after. We do not presently think that likely.
Risks and Open Items

items: Missing amendment. The plan document refers to an amendment that is not in the material we received. It does not block the model, because the recent valuations appear to have valued the provisions as we understand them. It blocks the report, because we will not describe provisions we have not read. This is the item most likely to move the delivery date and it sits with your recordkeeper, not with us.
Custodian statements for one quarter. Outstanding. Low risk. The asset roll-forward completes once they arrive and the projections do not depend on them.

The equity question. It was not resolved at the working session and it will not resolve itself. It is a decision for Wells, Walsh and Martinez and not an actuarial one. We will show the consequences of each candidate for it and we will not choose for you.

Expectations about stability. Contribution stability, ranked second, is bought with contributions that are higher on average in the good years. That is arithmetic rather than opinion. If the firm sees the figure and finds it unacceptable, the objectives statement changes and the candidates change with it. That is an ordinary outcome at this stage and a disruptive one later, which is why we want the ranking tested against real figures before the policy is drafted.

Data quality generally. All of the above rests on information your office and your service providers have furnished. We have reviewed it for internal consistency and general reasonableness. We have not audited it, and we do not.

blocks.text: David Sherman and I are available to walk through any of this by phone. If the missing amendment can be produced in the next two weeks, we do not at present see anything between here and delivery on the schedule we set.
